question: If the rules don't cover something in Swords & Wizardry, are we supposed to find the closest matching rule, or just let the referee make a call?
answer: You let the referee make the call. Swords & Wizardry is a free-form game with few rules; the Referee is explicitly responsible for situations the rules don't cover and for making fair evaluations of what characters do. The rules are guidelines for things like combat, experience, movement, and healing—not a complete procedure for every situation. The game expects you and the Referee to work together: players say what their characters do, the Referee describes the world and what happens. The Referee's discretion overrides the rules; the book says to use common sense and that 'making up' a lot on the spot is normal. So you're not required to hunt for a closest-matching rule; the Referee can rule either by analogy to an existing rule or by fiat, and the table can use house rules. Finding a close rule is optional guidance, not an obligation.
expected_answer_summary: Let the referee make the call; rules are guidelines, Referee handles uncovered situations and discretion overrides.
_gold_note: Good candidate for HYDE vocabulary (referee discretion, free-form, common sense, improvisation).

question: How is treasure typically divided among the party in S&W?
answer: The Splitting the Take variant suggests totaling the gp value of the loot and dividing it equally among the participants, with each getting an equal share. Many Referees rule that treasure must be shared with NPCs getting either a full or half share depending on their level. It is also customary for fallen (dead) comrades to still get a share of the take, to be given to their next of kin.
expected_answer_summary: Total gp and divide equally; NPCs full or half share; fallen comrades get share for next of kin.

question: Are the S&W treasure tables mandatory?
answer: No. The treasure tables are merely guidelines. You can place specific items if you desire, omit gems, jewelry, or magic items and leave the treasure at coins only. The tables are there only to help when wanted.
expected_answer_summary: No. Tables are guidelines; you can place specific items or leave treasure as coins only.

question: What's the relationship between treasure value and experience in S&W?
answer: The monetary value of a treasure ought to be about 2–3 times the monster's value in experience points. Treasure is one of the ways the game reflects what a character has done and is used in awarding experience points.
expected_answer_summary: Treasure value should be about 2–3× monster XP value; treasure used for awarding XP.

question: Is there a mathematical formula or budget behind the class abilities in S&W White Box?
answer: No.  Swords & Wizardry White Box classes weren’t built from a modern balancing algorithm.  The game is a retroclone of 1974 D&D and its classes were designed to emulate the feel of OD&D rather than follow a point‑buy formula or equal power budget【781072687742388†L170-L185】.
expected_answer_summary: No.  Swords & Wizardry White Box classes weren’t built from a modern balancing algorithm.  The game is a retroclone of 1974 D&D and its classes were designed to emulate the feel of OD&D rather than follow a point‑buy formula or equal power budget【781072687742388†L170-L185】.

question: How often can a magic-user prepare new spells in Swords & Wizardry? Can they re-prepare spells during the day?
answer: Magic-users don't automatically know every spell—they have a spellbook that may only contain some of the spells on the standard lists. From that book they 'prepare' spells: they press a spell formula into memory, and that uses one of their daily 'slots' (how many slots they get is on the class table by level). Once a prepared spell is cast, it's gone from their mind and they have to prepare it again to cast it another time. They can prepare the same spell in multiple slots if they want (e.g. Magic Missile in two slots = two castings). When can they re-prepare during the day? In the normal combat sequence, they start preparing at the beginning of a round and cast when their side has initiative; in a later round they can spend that round preparing a different spell (or the same one again if they have an open slot). In the alternative combat sequence it's explicit: after the spell goes off at the start of the round, the caster may spend the rest of the round preparing a new spell—or moving, or even fighting. So yes, they can re-prepare during the day, round by round as the rules allow. One caveat: if the caster takes damage while preparing a spell, that spell is lost and they don't get to cast it. Spell scrolls found on adventures can be copied into the spellbook.
expected_answer_summary: Magic-users prepare from spellbook into slots; after casting they can re-prepare round by round as combat sequence allows; damage while preparing loses the spell.

question: In the Turn Undead table, what does a ‘T’ result mean compared to a number?
answer: A number on the Turn Undead table is the minimum roll on 1d20 needed to turn that undead; the cleric must meet or beat that number. The letter ‘T’ means the undead is automatically turned without rolling, while ‘D’ indicates automatic destruction.
expected_answer_summary: A number on the Turn Undead table is the minimum roll on 1d20 needed to turn that undead; the cleric must meet or beat that number. The letter ‘T’ means the undead is automatically turned without rolling, while ‘D’ indicates automatic destruction.

question: Does a first‑level cleric get to cast spells in Swords & Wizardry?
answer: No. In Swords & Wizardry a cleric does not gain spell slots until 2nd level.
_gold_note: Page 37 Turn Undead (Variant) text is below min_chars=100; main evidence is p.14 table.
expected_answer_summary: No. In Swords & Wizardry a cleric does not gain spell slots until 2nd level.

question: How do experience points and level advancement work in Swords & Wizardry?
answer: Characters earn experience points from defeating monsters and treasure. Each class has its own advancement table with the XP total needed per level: Cleric (p.14), Fighter (p.16), Magic-User (p.18), and Elf variant (p.22). S&W uses rounded advancement numbers; the Scale of Advancement section (p.65) explains how these totals work and that the referee may adjust the pace.
expected_answer_summary: Characters earn experience points from defeating monsters and treasure. Each class has its own advancement table with the XP total needed per level: Cleric (p.14), Fighter (p.16), Magic-User (p.18), and Elf variant (p.22). S&W uses rounded advancement numbers; the Scale of Advancement section (p.65) explains how these totals work and that the referee may adjust the pace.

question: How do race, class, and ability score bonuses work in Swords & Wizardry?
answer: S&W separates race and class: humans and non-humans choose a class independently, and non-humans (e.g. elves) may multiclass. Ability score bonuses use separate tables per attribute with relatively small bonuses; by default only fighters get strength bonuses to hit and damage. An optional rule (Universal Attribute Bonus) can extend attribute bonuses more broadly.
expected_answer_summary: S&W separates race and class: humans and non-humans choose a class independently, and non-humans (e.g. elves) may multiclass. Ability score bonuses use separate tables per attribute with relatively small bonuses; by default only fighters get strength bonuses to hit and damage. An optional rule (Universal Attribute Bonus) can extend attribute bonuses more broadly.

question: Can humans dual‑class in Swords & Wizardry?
answer: The book does not specify and emphasizes that the Referee is free to invent or allow other character classes. So the answer depends on your game's referee.
_gold_note: One primary gold chunk (p.14); p.8 (Rule Number One), p.19, and p.65 are supporting. P.8 states the Referee may modify the rules and gaps are left on purpose; p.65 adds referee-as-ultimate-judge.
expected_answer_summary: The book does not specify and emphasizes that the Referee is free to invent or allow other character classes. So the answer depends on your game's referee.

question: Does Swords & Wizardry impose minimum ability scores to qualify for a class?
answer: No.  S&W dispenses with minimum attribute requirements entirely.  Labyrinth Lord retains minimum scores for each class.  In S&W high attributes still grant positive experience bonuses (up to +15%), and high wisdom provides an XP bonus regardless of class【91111277217397†L219-L223】.
expected_answer_summary: No.  S&W dispenses with minimum attribute requirements entirely.  Labyrinth Lord retains minimum scores for each class.  In S&W high attributes still grant positive experience bonuses (up to +15%), and high wisdom provides an XP bonus regardless of class【91111277217397†L219-L223】.

question: How are starting spells determined for magic‑users in S&W compared to Labyrinth Lord?
answer: Labyrinth Lord magic‑users and elves begin play knowing two 1st‑level spells and one 2nd‑level spell.  In Swords & Wizardry, the magic‑user checks each of the eight basic 1st‑level spells against their Learn Spell chance to see which they know.  The character may end up with fewer than the maximum allowed by their Intelligence【91111277217397†L224-L228】.
expected_answer_summary: Labyrinth Lord magic‑users and elves begin play knowing two 1st‑level spells and one 2nd‑level spell.  In Swords & Wizardry, the magic‑user checks each of the eight basic 1st‑level spells against their Learn Spell chance to see which they know.  The character may end up with fewer than the maximum allowed by their Intelligence【91111277217397†L224-L228】.

question: What dice are used for thief skills and saving throws in S&W as compared to Labyrinth Lord?
answer: Labyrinth Lord uses a d20 roll for thief skill checks.  Swords & Wizardry resolves most saving throws and thief skills using percentile dice except for Hear Noise, which uses a d6【91111277217397†L229-L231】.
expected_answer_summary: Labyrinth Lord uses a d20 roll for thief skill checks.  Swords & Wizardry resolves most saving throws and thief skills using percentile dice except for Hear Noise, which uses a d6【91111277217397†L229-L231】.

question: How does the combat sequence in Swords & Wizardry differ from Labyrinth Lord’s phase system?
answer: Both games use group initiative, but Labyrinth Lord divides each round into phases (spell casting, missile attacks, melee, and movement) and completes all phases for one side before the other side acts.  Swords & Wizardry’s standard sequence resolves all actions for a side at once.  There is also an optional alternate sequence in S&W that handles actions by phase with order determined by Dexterity【91111277217397†L252-L260】.
expected_answer_summary: Both games use group initiative, but Labyrinth Lord divides each round into phases (spell casting, missile attacks, melee, and movement) and completes all phases for one side before the other side acts.  Swords & Wizardry’s standard sequence resolves all actions for a side at once.  There is also an optional alternate sequence in S&W that handles actions by phase with order determined by Dexterity【91111277217397†L252-L260】.

question: How does Swords & Wizardry handle characters at 0 hit points and healing compared to Labyrinth Lord?
answer: In Labyrinth Lord a creature dies when reduced to 0 hit points.  Swords & Wizardry treats 0 hp as unconsciousness: the character bleeds out at 1 hp per round and dies when negative hit points equal their level.  Healing also differs: LL characters regain 1d3 hp per full day of rest, while S&W characters regain 1 hp per day but heal to full after four weeks of rest【91111277217397†L291-L296】.
expected_answer_summary: In Labyrinth Lord a creature dies when reduced to 0 hit points.  Swords & Wizardry treats 0 hp as unconsciousness: the character bleeds out at 1 hp per round and dies when negative hit points equal their level.  Healing also differs: LL characters regain 1d3 hp per full day of rest, while S&W characters regain 1 hp per day but heal to full after four weeks of rest【91111277217397†L291-L296】.

question: What rule does Swords & Wizardry use for firing missile weapons into melee?
answer: When firing into a melee in S&W, a successful attack does not automatically hit your intended target.  Instead, the target of a hit is chosen randomly among all creatures in the melee, including allies.  Labyrinth Lord provides no explicit rule for firing into melee【91111277217397†L273-L275】.
expected_answer_summary: When firing into a melee in S&W, a successful attack does not automatically hit your intended target.  Instead, the target of a hit is chosen randomly among all creatures in the melee, including allies.  Labyrinth Lord provides no explicit rule for firing into melee【91111277217397†L273-L275】.

question: The S&W Light rules list the thief’s Armor Class as 12[7]; is this correct?
answer: No.  Tenkar’s Tavern clarified that the line for the thief’s AC is a typo: it should read AC 7[12] (descending AC first with ascending AC in brackets).  The reversed numbers in the printed S&W Light booklet are an error【319006729373824†L41-L47】.
expected_answer_summary: No.  Tenkar’s Tavern clarified that the line for the thief’s AC is a typo: it should read AC 7[12] (descending AC first with ascending AC in brackets).  The reversed numbers in the printed S&W Light booklet are an error【319006729373824†L41-L47】.

question: Does S&W Light assume readers already know the full Swords & Wizardry rules?
answer: No.  The four‑page S&W Light rules do not require prior knowledge of Swords & Wizardry.  They presume players are familiar with role‑playing games in general but there is no assumption that they have read other S&W rulebooks【319006729373824†L50-L57】.
expected_answer_summary: No.  The four‑page S&W Light rules do not require prior knowledge of Swords & Wizardry.  They presume players are familiar with role‑playing games in general but there is no assumption that they have read other S&W rulebooks【319006729373824†L50-L57】.

question: Why doesn’t S&W Light include a full table of ability score bonuses?
answer: Because the bonuses in S&W Light are very small.  Characters never gain more than a +1 bonus from an ability score in these streamlined rules, so a table was omitted to save space【319006729373824†L59-L65】.
expected_answer_summary: Because the bonuses in S&W Light are very small.  Characters never gain more than a +1 bonus from an ability score in these streamlined rules, so a table was omitted to save space【319006729373824†L59-L65】.

question: What die is used for Hit Dice in S&W Light?  The book doesn’t seem to say.
answer: S&W Light uses only d6s and d20s.  Hit Dice are d6s, so a monster or character with 3 HD rolls three six‑sided dice for hit points.  ‘HD’ simply means the number of d6s rolled【319006729373824†L68-L77】.
expected_answer_summary: S&W Light uses only d6s and d20s.  Hit Dice are d6s, so a monster or character with 3 HD rolls three six‑sided dice for hit points.  ‘HD’ simply means the number of d6s rolled【319006729373824†L68-L77】.

question: Do magic‑users in S&W Light start with a weapon?  If so, what?
answer: Yes.  The S&W Light magic‑user should receive a dagger or staff as a starting weapon.  The equipment section omits this in error, but the clarification states that the magic‑user should get one of those simple weapons【319006729373824†L81-L86】.
expected_answer_summary: Yes.  The S&W Light magic‑user should receive a dagger or staff as a starting weapon.  The equipment section omits this in error, but the clarification states that the magic‑user should get one of those simple weapons【319006729373824†L81-L86】.

question: Does casting a spell in S&W Light require a roll or skill test?
answer: No.  Casting a spell in S&W Light does not require any kind of success roll.  A spell only fails or is lost if the caster is interrupted or takes damage during casting【319006729373824†L88-L93】.
expected_answer_summary: No.  Casting a spell in S&W Light does not require any kind of success roll.  A spell only fails or is lost if the caster is interrupted or takes damage during casting【319006729373824†L88-L93】.

question: How do surprise and initiative work in Swords & Wizardry Complete?
answer: If one side is surprised, the other side gets a free action or attack before initiative is rolled.  After this surprise round, initiative is determined normally and each side acts in order.  Surprise therefore grants a single free action rather than an entire round【371582203323234†L39-L54】.
expected_answer_summary: If one side is surprised, the other side gets a free action or attack before initiative is rolled.  After this surprise round, initiative is determined normally and each side acts in order.  Surprise therefore grants a single free action rather than an entire round【371582203323234†L39-L54】.

question: Can a character fire a missile weapon and make a melee attack in the same round in S&W Complete?
answer: No.  The combat sequence in S&W Complete allows each combatant one action per round.  A character may move and fire a missile weapon (or move and fight in melee) but cannot both fire a missile and make a melee attack in the same round.  Only a half‑move can be combined with a single attack【371582203323234†L56-L69】.
expected_answer_summary: No.  The combat sequence in S&W Complete allows each combatant one action per round.  A character may move and fire a missile weapon (or move and fight in melee) but cannot both fire a missile and make a melee attack in the same round.  Only a half‑move can be combined with a single attack【371582203323234†L56-L69】.

question: What happens to a spell being cast if the caster takes damage in Swords & Wizardry Complete?
answer: If a spellcaster is struck or otherwise disrupted while casting a spell in S&W Complete, the spell is lost for the day.  The caster must prepare it again before it can be used【371582203323234†L71-L83】.
expected_answer_summary: If a spellcaster is struck or otherwise disrupted while casting a spell in S&W Complete, the spell is lost for the day.  The caster must prepare it again before it can be used【371582203323234†L71-L83】.

question: Is Swords & Wizardry compatible with 1st‑edition AD&D modules and other OSR adventures?
answer: Yes.  Swords & Wizardry was designed to be compatible with original and first‑edition D&D material.  Players report that it runs old modules well.  Other retroclones like OSRIC or Labyrinth Lord may hew even more closely to AD&D, but converting material to S&W is straightforward【338419238901263†L185-L213】.
expected_answer_summary: Yes.  Swords & Wizardry was designed to be compatible with original and first‑edition D&D material.  Players report that it runs old modules well.  Other retroclones like OSRIC or Labyrinth Lord may hew even more closely to AD&D, but converting material to S&W is straightforward【338419238901263†L185-L213】.
